_note: Content for the buying guide page. 'intro' = short text in the site header. 'sections' = numbered sections. 'body' = array of paragraphs. 'list' = optional bullet list. 'example' = callout box on the right.
intro: Font licensing used to be simple: one typeface, one price, done. Today foundries charge by company size, pageviews, or app count, add surcharges for logo use, and separate web from desktop. Staying on top of this avoids costly surprises and licensing gaps.

sections.title: What you buy — and what you don’t
body: Buying a font doesn’t transfer ownership — it grants a license: the right to use the typeface in a specific way. The foundry retains ownership of the design.
What is permitted is defined in the End User License Agreement, the EULA — the most important document when purchasing. A license covers certain media and is often tied to scale or reach.

example.label: Example
example.t: A desktop license lets you set a poster. It does not automatically allow you to embed the same font via @font-face on a website — for that you need a web license.

sections.title: The common license types
body: Most foundries divide their offering by use case. Which license you need depends on where the typeface will ultimately appear.
list: Desktop / Print — installed on computers, for print and static layouts
Web — embedded as a webfont via @font-face
App / Game — embedded in an application
ePub — embedded in electronic publications
Broadcast / Video — motion content, from YouTube to broadcast TV

example.label: Example
example.t: Some foundries sell each type separately, others bundle them. When in doubt, a license only covers what is explicitly stated.

sections.title: How pricing is calculated
body: The hardest part of comparison: every foundry uses a different pricing basis. The same term — desktop license — can mean very different prices.
list: Per workstation — price scales with the number of computers
By company size — based on the client's headcount
By pageviews — web licenses tiered by monthly page views
Flat fee — one price, unlimited use within the licensed medium

example.label: Example — the common case
example.t: At most foundries, every person working with the font needs their own license — billed per workstation. Lineto, for example, licenses per user. Models where only the end client holds the license and the studio is covered are the exception.

sections.title: Client, agency, or project?
body: An often overlooked question: who holds the license? This isn’t just a formality — it determines who can continue using the font when the project changes hands.
Typically, the licensee uses the font themselves. Some foundries require the end client to hold the license, with the studio merely covered under it.

example.label: Example
example.t: A studio designs a brand identity. Two years later a different agency is brought in to continue the campaign. If the license was in the client's name, they can authorise the second agency. If it was only in the first studio's name, the client has no rights.

sections.title: Extra costs that catch you off guard
body: Beyond the base license, certain uses frequently cost extra or require a separate license. Checking early leads to cleaner project calculations.
list: Logo / Wordmark — often subject to surcharge or a separate license
Wayfinding / Signage systems — sometimes treated separately
Packaging / Merchandise — products intended for sale
Modification — editing the font file isn't always permitted
AI Training — many EULAs prohibit it explicitly

example.label: Example
example.t: A logo is designed once but used for decades. That's precisely why many foundries treat logo use as its own paid category.

sections.title: Trials, student discounts and more
body: Not every use requires full price — and not every early project phase needs a license at all.
list: Trial fonts — free test fonts, usually for pitching only
Student discount — widespread, often for personal projects only
Non-profit / Cultural — reduced pricing available

example.label: Rule of thumb
example.t: A license is only required once something is published — printed, gone live, shipped. Until then, trial fonts are usually sufficient.

sections.title: Check before you buy
body: Five questions to answer before every font purchase:
list: In which media will the font appear — does the license cover all of them?
Who holds the license — client, studio, or project?
How is the price calculated — can that change later?
Are there special cases — logo, signage, packaging?
Is it perpetual or a subscription, and is the license transferable?

example.label: Note
example.t: The EULA of each foundry is always the authoritative document. This guide doesn’t replace it — it helps you read it.
